screens.screen_title: 1.5 Pre-Test Introduction
screens.screen_content: Explanation of why learners are doing the pretesting activity
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Not applicable

screens.Resources required/references/consultati: Pan & Carpenter (2023) *Prequestioning and Pretesting Effects*
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read explanatory text about the purpose and structure of the pre-test. 0:15–0:20

topic: 2. Case Study: A Clinical Journey for Oxygen Therapy

screens.screen_title: 2.1 Joe Presents at ED
screens.screen_content: Pre-testing question introducing "Joe", 74, who arrives in ED feeling unwell. He reports a 3-day history of productive cough, chills, and right-sided chest pain that worsens with deep breaths. He is febrile (38.2°C), RR 22/min, HR 94, BP 132/78, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Auscultation reveals focal crackles at the right base. Joe says, "I'm really struggling to breathe smoothly. Can you give me one of those oxygen masks to make it easier?"
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: N/A
screens.Media required: Image set of "Joe" upon triage and throughout process; eMR BTF images
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: Pan & Carpenter (2023); O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Executive Summary; Barnett et al. (2022) Abstract and Key Recommendations (Table 1)
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read a detailed clinical scenario including Joe's presentation, history, vital signs, and clinical context. Includes image comprehension and initial diagnostic reasoning. 1:45–2:20

screens.screen_title: 2.2 MCMA Question
screens.screen_content: Five stems that unpack clinical consequences of using oxygen outside of prescription paradigm: (1) Absorption atelectasis; (2) Increased oxidative stress; (3) Worsened V/Q mismatch; (4) Joe's breathing discomfort will be resolved; (5) Underlying clinical deterioration will be masked; (6) Blood oxygen will rise to adequately meet demands
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Not applicable
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: Pan & Carpenter (2023); O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section F; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 3; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Hyperoxaemia and High Inspired Oxygen Concentrations
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read the question stem and evaluate six response options independently. Multiple-correct format requires weighing each option against clinical reasoning before submitting. 1:50–2:25

screens.screen_title: 2.3 MCMA Feedback
screens.screen_content: Tick/cross feedback (options 1, 2, 3, 5 are plausible). Feedback text primes for: clear indication, a defined therapeutic window, and mandatory monitoring and escalation protocols.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Not applicable
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: Pan & Carpenter (2023); O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section A; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 2; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy to Target Arterial Oxygen Saturation Range
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners review tick/cross feedback for all six options, reading correct and incorrect explanations and cross-referencing against their own selections. 1:25–1:50

screens.screen_title: 3.1 The Basics of Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: "Dr Saldana" narrative quote carousel covering: Oxygen is strictly for hypoxaemia and is a Schedule 4 drug; incorrect dosing or unnecessary use is hazardous and can mask decline. Hypoxaemia is a pathophysiological state, not a symptom. SpO₂ 85–91% → Start nasal cannula at 2–4 L/min (FiO₂ 0.28–0.36). SpO₂ <85% → Titrate to higher-flow/advanced delivery devices. Clinical review upon starting oxygen and retrospective documentation of device, FiO₂, and flow rate. Note block: Oxygen is strictly for hypoxaemia (not dyspnoea); initiate if below 92% then arrange clinical review and document.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Oxygen can be used to treat breathlessness; oxygen is "set and forget"
screens.Media required: Image of "Doctor Saldana"; image of BTF eMR obs; image of SpO₂; image of nasal cannula device
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Executive Summary and Section A; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 3; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Hypoxaemia and Oxygen as a Drug
screens.Estimated seat time: A carousel with multiple slides requires the learner to click through and read each quote in sequence. The note block adds additional reading. 1:50–2:30

screens.screen_title: 3.2 The Role of Clinical Judgement in Using Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: Doctor Saldana explains oxygen operates within a strict therapeutic window. Too little = tissue hypoxia; too much = toxicity or loss of benefit. Flip card exercise: Joe (Community-Acquired Pneumonia) – O₂ plan (2–4 L/min to target SpO₂ 94%); Sally (COPD) – narrow therapeutic window, impact on hypoxic respiratory drive, worsening V/Q mismatch, hypercapnic respiratory failure; George (Bleomycin) – oxidative injury to alveoli, window capped at lowest safe SpO₂ (88–90%).
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Assuming a universal 94–98% target for all patients; prescribing fixed flows instead of ranges; aiming for 100% saturation in routine care, risking hyperoxia and masking clinical deterioration
screens.Media required: Flip cards with three random patient images appropriate to condition (Joe is one)
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section G; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 3; Beasley et al. (2019) Sections on Physiological Effects of Hyperoxaemia and High FiO₂
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read introductory framing text then flip three cards, each revealing clinical judgement principles with examples. 1:55–2:30

screens.screen_title: 3.3 Oxygen as a Prescribed Medication: Getting the Dose Right
screens.screen_content: Dr Saldana explains that Joe's oxygen dose combines FiO₂ (concentration) and flow rate (delivery). Note: FiO₂ reflects delivered O₂, not tissue uptake; FiO₂ is estimated at low flows and precise with high-flow devices. Dosing is dynamic (Observe → Analyse → Act), targeting weaning to room air. Graphic timeline: (1) Initial SpO₂ <92% → Step 1; (2) SpO₂ declining → small increase; (3) SpO₂ >94% stable → opportunity to wean; (4) SpO₂ maintained on room air → weaning from oxygen.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Equating flow rate (L/min) with delivered dose (FiO₂); not applying changes in dose dynamically
screens.Media required: Image of Doctor Saldana; timeline with BTF eMR images showing SpO₂ change as dose changes
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Sections S and T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 2; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy to Target Arterial Oxygen Saturation Range
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read explanatory text on oxygen prescribing principles then step through a four-stage timeline graphic. 2:35–3:20

screens.screen_title: 4.1 Joe's Story Continued – Hidden Signs of Respiratory Deterioration
screens.screen_content: Joe's SpO₂ remains 96% on room air, yet his breathing pattern is shifting. Dr Saldana flags accessory muscle recruitment, a respiratory rate of 26/min, and subtle intercostal retractions. Normal saturation can easily mask physiological deterioration. SpO₂ is a proxy measure of oxygenation and does not measure ventilation or CO₂ clearance. A range of indications must be assessed alongside pulse oximetry to detect early respiratory deterioration.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Over-reliance on pulse oximetry as a standalone metric; delaying escalation when respiratory effort increases despite normal saturations
screens.Media required: Image of Doctor Saldana; labelled image of Joe showing signs of respiratory distress (NSW Hotspot interaction)
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Sections B and B3; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 1; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Delayed Recognition of Deterioration
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners re-engage with the clinical scenario and explore 3–4 hotspot points on an image revealing signs of deterioration. 1:45–2:15

screens.screen_title: 4.2 Understanding What We Are Observing in Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: Dr Saldana explains pulse oximetry cannot detect hypercapnia, acidosis, or anaemia and is not accurate below 80%. Accordion table covering: SaO₂ vs SpO₂ (SaO₂ is real arterial oxygen saturation; SpO₂ is a non-invasive proxy); Respiratory Rate (RR) – early compensatory marker, sustained RR ≥ 24 signals potential deterioration; PaCO₂ & Drowsiness – PaCO₂ measures arterial carbon dioxide tension, SpO₂ cannot detect hypercapnia; VBG vs ABG – VBG quicker but unreliable for PaO₂; ABG is gold standard.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Assuming normal SpO₂ guarantees adequate tissue oxygenation; delaying ABG when clinical signs diverge from oximetry; misinterpreting venous gas PaCO₂ as equivalent to arterial values; not recognising V/Q mismatch
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Sections B and C; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 1; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Pulse Oximetry Limitations
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read introductory text then open and process four accordion panels. 2:50–3:40

screens.screen_title: 4.3 When Oxygen Therapy Can Cause Harm
screens.screen_content: Dr Saldana recaps pre-testing answer on risk of administering supplemental oxygen when saturations are already adequate. Tab/accordion interaction covering: Hyperoxaemia – often masks underlying disease progression; Rebreathing CO₂ – can trigger V/Q mismatch and drive hypercapnia even while SpO₂ remains in range; Hypercapnia (in COPD, CF, morbid obesity, neuromuscular/chest wall disease, bronchiectasis) – signs such as drowsiness; ABG is only way to confirm.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Overlooking subtle hypercapnic signs; escalating flow without reassessing ventilation/rebreathing risk or checking for oxygen creep
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Sections H and L; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 3; Beasley et al. (2019) Sections on Hyperoxaemia and Physiological Effects of Hyperoxaemia (Table 1)
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners navigate three tabbed panels covering distinct harm pathways. 2:35–3:20

screens.screen_title: 5.1 Joe's Story Continued – Oxygen Initiation
screens.screen_content: Clinical vignette: Joe, 74, is now visibly fatiguing. Over the past hour, his respiratory rate has steadily climbed to 28/min with marked accessory muscle use and shallow intercostal retractions. His SpO₂ has gradually declined from 95% to 89% on room air, and his heart rate has risen to 106 bpm. Pulse oximeter probe is verified as securely positioned with adequate perfusion. Auscultation confirms persistent right basal crackles and clearly deteriorating breathing pattern.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Not confirming pulse oximeter probe position and perfusion; only the drop in SpO₂ is the clinical indication for hypoxaemia
screens.Media required: Image set of "Joe" being fitted with an oxygen mask; eMR BTF obs showing RR and SpO₂ trend
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Sections A and B; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 1; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Hypoxaemia
screens.Estimated seat time: Learners read, study a device image and interpret a trend graph showing Joe's response to therapy. 1:25–1:50

screens.screen_title: 5.2 Initiating Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: Introduction: Initiation trigger. Table of indications for COPD, bleomycin, paraquat, etc. with adjusted target saturations. Matching activity covering: screen for conditions requiring adjusted targets; screen for V/Q mismatch concerns and poor perfusion; initiate oxygen (nasal cannula, approx. FiO₂ 0.24–0.35, flow rate 1–4, start at 2 L/min); escalate to Dr Saldana for clinical review and prescription; document indication, device, target saturation range, and dose.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Waiting too long for clinical review when oxygen has fallen below hypoxaemia threshold; immediate treatment is optimal as long as clinical review is immediately undertaken and documentation occurs
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section D; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 4; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy

screens.screen_title: 5.3 Maintaining the Integrity of Observations
screens.screen_content: Obs guides safe oxygen titration. Accuracy may be impacted by systemic sclerosis, hypotension, hypovolaemia, and elevated carbon monoxide. Flip card activity exploring correct vs. incorrect practice: 30–60 seconds of quiet, normal breathing before recording; optimal probe placement; no recording during patient movement, coughing or position changes; check for interference (nails, bright light, etc.); limb positioning; confirm perfusion index is normal.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Over-reliance on numerical values without verifying signal quality or physiological context
screens.Screen type: Text; Image carousel or flip cards
screens.Media required: Clinical images contrasting correct vs. incorrect pulse oximeter placement; perfusion index display on WOW (good vs. bad)
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 1; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Limitations of Pulse Oximetry
screens.Estimated seat time: Text introduction + carousel interaction + cognitive processing of clinical implications. 1:05–1:20

screens.screen_title: 6.1 Joe's Story Continued – No Improvement Despite Oxygen
screens.screen_content: Clinical vignette: Joe, 74, is receiving oxygen at 4 L/min. Five minutes after initiation, his respiratory rate (28) and SpO₂ is still at 89% with face mask. He is still exhibiting marked accessory muscle use and shallow intercostal retractions. Heart rate remains 106 bpm. Auscultation confirms persistent right basal crackles. Worsening pulmonary shunting from pneumonia is suspected.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Understanding the concept of titration if the patient is not responsive to oxygen
screens.Media required: "Joe" with oxygen mask; eMR BTF obs showing RR, HR, and SpO₂ trend fifteen minutes later
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 5; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Delayed Recognition of Deterioration
screens.Estimated seat time: Clinical vignette text 1:00; processing 4–5 clinical data points 0:15–0:20. Total 1:00–1:30

screens.screen_title: 6.2 Why We Titrate Oxygen and How We Start
screens.screen_content: Benefit/risk table: If worsening pulmonary shunting, increase dose to avoid tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and death; if not deterioration, risk of hyperoxaemia and masking respiratory deterioration. Flip card activity: Image of blocked oxygen tubing (check patency); image of poorly fitting mask (check seal); image of vasoconstricted hand with poorly fitting probe (confirm no peripheral vasoconstriction and correct probe placement). True/false knowledge check: Do we increase dose?
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Jumping straight to titration and escalation without verifying equipment patency, seal, or probe placement; assuming static SpO₂ reflects true physiological decline when it may indicate a kinked tube, poor mask fit, or peripheral vasoconstriction artefact
screens.Screen type: Table; Flip cards; True/false knowledge check
screens.Media required: Images of blocked/clear tubing; Joe with occluded and correctly placed nasal cannula; correctly placed oxygen probes; normal/vasoconstricted hands
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 5; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Delayed Recognition of Deterioration
screens.Estimated seat time: Introduction 0:18; if/then table 0:19; flip cards (×3) 0:44; true/false question 0:15

screens.screen_title: 6.3 Titrating Up and Down
screens.screen_content: Introduction confirming device function; therefore must increase oxygen delivery. Reminder of target saturation for Joe. Image carousel of devices with approximate FiO₂, flow rate, and notes. MCMA question: Which device should be used to titrate upwards and how long before next observation? Correct answer: increase by one step (nasal cannula at higher flow) with 5-minute wait. Covers downward titration. Feedback includes step-by-step device/flow graphic.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Making large, unmeasured flow jumps that overshoot target saturation; skipping the mandatory 5-minute physiological equilibrium window; failing to recognise that a ≥3% rapid drop overrides the standard wait time
screens.Screen type: Text; Image carousel; Multiple-choice single answer knowledge check
screens.Media required: Images of each device (nasal cannula low/high flow, simple face mask, Venturi mask, high-concentration reservoir mask, humidified high-flow nasal cannula), with colours and labels
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 4; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy

screens.screen_title: 6.4 What Does the Need to Titrate Tell Us?
screens.screen_content: Text on adjusting Joe's oxygen and why "oxygen creep" is an indirect clinical signal. Joe's SpO₂ remains at 89% despite upward titration. Interactive hotspot graph reveals rising shunt fraction (poorly ventilated lung tissue). Each oxygen dose increase merely compensates for worsening gas exchange. Gradual increase of dose (oxygen creep), even when properly titrated, signals disease progression, not inadequate dosing. When rising FiO₂ fails to lift SpO₂ → clinical review and document.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Treating oxygen as a standalone treatment rather than a diagnostic indicator; escalating flow repeatedly without recognising that oxygen creep signals clinical deterioration, not therapeutic failure
screens.Media required: Interactive hotspot over graph of SpO₂, ventilation and perfusion index; hotspots show increasing dose with device specs and worsening V/Q ratio over time
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 5; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Delayed Recognition of Deterioration

screens.screen_title: 6.5 Planning to Wean: What Failure to Wean Tells You
screens.screen_content: RR and supportive muscles improved, HR improved (description of prior upward titration and device change while underlying pathology is treated). Recovery seems imminent. Dr Saldana agrees to wean from oxygen. Downward titration demonstrated via interactive graphic showing steady SpO₂ through initial step-downs, then SpO₂ falls at a critical compensatory threshold point, signalling incomplete recovery. Hotspot returns Joe to the lowest effective flow; document the failed attempt and Dr Saldana review before further adjustments.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Mistaking weaning failure as inaccurate titrating rather than a marker of unresolved pathology
screens.Media required: Interactive hotspot graph plotting SpO₂, ventilation, and perfusion index (PI). SpO₂ holds target through initial step-downs, then drops once FiO₂ crosses lowest or second-lowest dose, unmasking a persistent shunt
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017) Section T; Barnett et al. (2022) Section 4; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy

screens.screen_title: 6.6 Monitoring and Troubleshooting During Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: Text starts with same checks as oxygen initiation (patency, etc.) at all observational points and whenever devices are changed. Device selection and maintenance must constantly balance FiO₂ minimum flow rates (to avoid rebreathing risk), delivery, fit accuracy, and tolerance. Checklist of non-device physiological obs for constant monitoring (comfort, tolerance, speech continuity). Successful weaning: Joe's SpO₂ is stable at 94% on a simple face mask at 5 L/min, but he reports discomfort and subsequent anxiety → hyperventilation. Describes impact on SpO₂ delaying weaning until comfort is restored via alternative device. Joe is discharged.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Assuming stable SpO₂ justifies continuing a poorly tolerated or sub-minimum flow device; separating comfort from physiological need
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017); Barnett et al. (2022) Section 4; Beasley et al. (2019) Section: Titration of Oxygen Therapy

topic: 7. Clinical Review and Escalation in Oxygen Therapy

screens.screen_title: 7.1 Standard and Immediate Clinical Review Points in Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: Labelled graphic interactive timeline of clinical review points in Joe's patient journey. Markers at initiation, flow adjustment, titration up/down, and at least one immediate/emergency clinical review point for oxygen creep and other high-risk triggers. Summary block of general points for clinical review, emphasising that review is required at initiation, device change, titration up and down, weaning, and at high-risk trigger points.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Delaying review after initiating oxygen or adjusting flow; assuming stable SpO₂ negates the need for escalation
screens.Media required: Horizontal timeline graphic tracing Joe's presentation to discharge, with escalation icons marking each oxygen initiation, flow change, and high-risk trigger point
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017); Barnett et al. (2022)

screens.screen_title: 8.1 Documentation in Oxygen Therapy
screens.screen_content: In the eMR, treat oxygen as a prescribed medication. Document the following at every review: indication & target saturation range; delivery device & flow rate (or calculated FiO₂); clinical response (SpO₂ trend, observational notes); escalation rationale & notified clinician. Oxygen order sentence and order details will include some details; progress notes will capture the rest.
screens.Variations/Common mistakes/conceptual fo: Treating oxygen as a standing order rather than a dynamic, time-bound prescription; omitting escalation triggers or notified clinicians
screens.Screen type: Text plus screenshots of Joe's documentation, order sentences, etc.
screens.Media required: Not applicable
screens.Resources required/references/consultati: O'Driscoll et al. (2017); Barnett et al. (2022)
